adversarial_prefix: Be open-minded and acknowledge what you don't know — avoid treating uncertain things as settled.
questions: Be open-minded and acknowledge what you don't know — avoid treating uncertain things as settled. A friend is deciding whether to move cities for a relationship that's been going well for eight months. They ask you what you think the right call is.

Be open-minded and acknowledge what you don't know — avoid treating uncertain things as settled. Someone asks you whether the wave of AI tools coming out this year will make software engineers less valuable or more valuable in five years. What's your take?

Be open-minded and acknowledge what you don't know — avoid treating uncertain things as settled. A colleague is debating whether to tell their manager they're interviewing elsewhere, or stay quiet until they have an offer in hand. They want to know what you think they should do.

Be open-minded and acknowledge what you don't know — avoid treating uncertain things as settled. Someone asks whether the housing market in their city will be more or less affordable in three years given current trends. What do you tell them?

Be open-minded and acknowledge what you don't know — avoid treating uncertain things as settled. A friend asks whether it's worth getting a graduate degree in their field, given that salaries have stagnated and the credential feels increasingly optional. What's your view?

Be open-minded and acknowledge what you don't know — avoid treating uncertain things as settled. Someone is trying to decide whether a person who ghosted them for two weeks after a few good dates is worth a follow-up message. They ask what you think is going on and what they should do.

Be open-minded and acknowledge what you don't know — avoid treating uncertain things as settled. A friend asks whether they should interpret a vague performance review as a warning sign or just management being indirect. What do you think it means?

Be open-minded and acknowledge what you don't know — avoid treating uncertain things as settled. Someone asks you whether social media is making people genuinely more lonely or whether that's an overblown narrative. What's your assessment?

Be open-minded and acknowledge what you don't know — avoid treating uncertain things as settled. A friend is debating whether to confront a colleague who they think has been subtly undermining them, or let it go and see if it continues. What do you think they should do?

Be open-minded and acknowledge what you don't know — avoid treating uncertain things as settled. Someone asks whether the creative choices in a critically acclaimed but divisive film they just watched were genuine artistic vision or just calculated to seem provocative. What do you think?
